content.text: Supplying spoken language or sound as input to AI models unlocks a class of pipelines that bypass manual transcription and operate directly on the richest channel humans use to communicate.

refers to the practice of sending audio data — live streams, recorded files, phone calls, or extracted video tracks — to speech-capable models together with text instructions that steer transcription, analysis, classification, or extraction tasks.

Audio prompting is the multimodal pattern where an audio payload (a file or byte stream) is included alongside a text prompt when invoking a model that accepts speech input. The model receives both modalities simultaneously — the sound and the instruction — and produces a text-based (or structured) output without requiring a separate, manually wired transcription step.
Two broad implementation strategies exist. In the
, a dedicated speech-to-text (STT) engine converts audio to a transcript first, and the transcript is fed to a standard language model as part of the text prompt. In the

, a multimodal model ingests the raw audio waveform or encoded audio bytes directly and processes audio and text in a unified forward pass — the model itself handles the acoustic understanding internally.

The key design choices in an audio prompt are: the audio encoding format and sample rate handed to the model, the language or locale hint, the diarization instruction (label speakers or not), the post-transcription task (summarize, classify, extract structured fields), and the output schema. These choices, expressed as text instructions bundled with the audio, constitute the prompt.

Voice is the primary communication channel for support calls, field-service dispatches, medical consultations, legal depositions, and sales conversations. Organizations accumulate hundreds of thousands of hours of recordings that carry insights — unresolved customer pain points, compliance violations, churn signals — but remain locked in audio because manual review is prohibitively expensive.

Audio prompting changes the economics. A one-hour support call that costs $15 to manually review can be processed by an API in under two minutes for a fraction of a cent per second of audio. More important, the output is not just a transcript: with a well-crafted prompt the model simultaneously transcribes, identifies the issue category, extracts the resolution, flags follow-up actions, and writes a CRM-ready summary — all in one model call.

For real-time applications — voice assistants, live captioning, meeting co-pilots — audio prompting enables sub-second response latency to spoken questions. The user speaks; the agent hears, reasons, and responds without the user ever typing. This creates interaction patterns impossible in text-only interfaces and opens AI to users with accessibility needs or in hands-free environments.
Transcription, extraction, and reasoning collapse into a single API round-trip, eliminating brittle multi-service orchestration.
Native audio models can detect tone, urgency, hesitation, and emotion from the waveform itself — signals lost after STT conversion.
Speaker-separated transcripts ("Agent:", "Customer:") are produced by the same model call rather than a dedicated diarization service.
The prompt can specify any output shape — raw transcript, timestamped SRT, JSON with named fields, or a prose summary — without post-processing code.
Multilingual models transcribe and reason in the detected or specified language without locale-specific pipelines.
Streaming audio APIs deliver partial transcripts and model responses before the speaker finishes, enabling responsive voice interfaces.
Automated audio analysis at millions of calls per month is orders of magnitude cheaper than human review or complex custom ML pipelines.
Audio prompting is not a silver bullet. Several limitations require deliberate architectural decisions.

Audio consumes far more tokens than equivalent text. A five-minute call at 16 kHz mono uses roughly 4.8 MB and may occupy tens of thousands of tokens, forcing chunking strategies for longer recordings.
Background noise, low-quality microphones, heavy accents, and overlapping speakers all raise word error rate. Unlike text, you cannot 'spell-check' audio before submission.
Higher per-call cost than pure text:
Audio tokens are priced at a premium over text tokens. Processing hours of meeting audio at scale can exceed budget expectations if not modeled carefully.
Large audio files must be uploaded, processed, and decoded before the model starts generating. For long recordings this can add seconds to minutes of wall-clock delay.
Unlike document prompts with retrievable text, audio content cannot be directly 'retrieved' or diffed. The model must hold the full audio in context, limiting dynamic retrieval patterns.
Models occasionally substitute plausible-sounding words for unclear audio, especially for names, jargon, and acronyms.

Audio often contains PII (names, account numbers spoken aloud), biometric voiceprint data, and HIPAA- or GDPR-regulated content. Sending raw recordings to third-party APIs requires careful data-processing agreements.

Before a model processes audio, the raw signal must be encoded into a format the API accepts. Common encodings are PCM WAV (lossless, large), MP3 (lossy, compressed), FLAC (lossless, compressed), and Opus (low-latency streaming). Most cloud APIs accept Base64-encoded audio bytes embedded in a JSON payload or as a multipart form-data upload. Streaming APIs accept chunked WebSocket or HTTP/2 frames.

Sample rate matters. STT models are typically trained at 16 kHz mono; sending 44.1 kHz stereo increases data volume without improving quality. A preprocessing step that resamples to 16 kHz mono is almost always worthwhile.

, an STT engine (e.g., OpenAI Whisper, Deepgram Nova, Google Speech-to-Text) converts audio to text with timestamps and optional diarization. The transcript becomes part of the text prompt sent to a language model. This approach separates concerns, lets each component be best-of-breed, and keeps LLM token costs low because text is far cheaper than audio tokens.

, a multimodal model such as GPT-4o Audio Preview or Gemini 1.5 Pro receives the raw audio alongside text instructions. The model's internal audio encoder (typically a Whisper-like transformer) produces acoustic embeddings that are fused with text token embeddings before the decoder. This allows the model to reason over prosody and paralinguistics — stress patterns, pauses, laughter — that are discarded in a transcript.

Most models cap audio context. A 60-minute call cannot be sent as one unit. The standard strategy is to split audio into overlapping segments (e.g., 5-minute chunks with a 15-second overlap at boundaries), process each chunk independently with a prompt that includes context from the prior chunk's summary, then merge outputs. The overlap prevents losing information at segment boundaries where sentences are cut mid-utterance.
is the overlap window. For a 60-minute recording with 5-minute chunks and 15-second overlaps, this yields approximately 12 chunks.

An effective audio prompt has three parts: (1) a system instruction specifying the model's role and output format, (2) any relevant prior context (previous transcript chunk summary, CRM record, caller history), and (3) the audio payload with a task instruction. Specifying the expected output schema — a JSON object with named fields — dramatically improves downstream parseability compared to free-form prose responses.
The diagram below shows the complete audio prompting pipeline, from raw input sources through preprocessing and the model layer to the structured outputs consumed by downstream applications.

attrs.alt: Audio Prompting Pipeline: input sources → preprocessor → STT or native LLM with prompt context → structured outputs

content.text: The key insight is that the model layer has two valid paths: a dedicated STT engine feeding a text LLM (the pipeline path, green), or a native multimodal LLM processing audio directly (purple). Prompt context (system instructions, output schema, prior chunk summary) is always injected as text regardless of which path is chosen.
Call center analytics — transcribe, classify intent, extract resolution, score agent performance at scale.
Meeting assistants — real-time or post-meeting summarization, action item extraction, attendee attribution.
Voice interfaces — hands-free AI assistants in automotive, healthcare, or industrial settings.
Medical documentation — converting dictated clinical notes to structured EHR fields, reducing physician documentation time.
Legal transcription — depositions, hearings, and interviews where accurate speaker attribution matters.
Media indexing — making podcast and video archives searchable by transcribing and extracting topics.
If a transcript already exists, feed text directly — audio tokens are more expensive and add no value when the acoustic signal is irrelevant.
When audio quality is below a usable threshold (heavy noise, extremely strong accent, non-standard codec artifacts) — invest in preprocessing or higher-quality capture first.
For tasks that require real-time sub-100ms response on commodity hardware — on-device STT is more appropriate than cloud audio LLMs.
When PII/biometric data governance prohibits sending voice recordings to third-party APIs — use on-premise STT and send only sanitized transcripts to cloud LLMs.
Implementation follows five concrete steps: prepare the audio, choose the model path, craft the prompt, call the API, and parse the response.
Prepare the audio: resample to 16 kHz mono, normalize amplitude, optionally apply noise reduction. Chunk recordings longer than the model's supported duration.
Choose path: for cost-sensitive batch processing, use a dedicated STT engine then a text LLM. For paralinguistic analysis or real-time voice, use a native audio LLM.
Craft the prompt: specify language, diarization requirements, output schema, and any context from prior chunks.
Call the API: submit audio as Base64-encoded bytes or a file reference. Handle rate limits and retry transient errors with exponential backoff.
Parse and validate: extract structured fields, validate required keys, handle null/missing fields gracefully, and persist results.
The following example shows a complete implementation using OpenAI's Whisper API for STT followed by GPT-4o for extraction — the pipeline approach:

import openai
import json
from pathlib import Path

client = openai.OpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from env

EXTRACTION_SCHEMA = {
    "issue_category": "string",
    "issue_summary": "string",
    "resolution": "string",
    "follow_up_required": "boolean",
    "follow_up_action": "string or null",
    "sentiment": "positive | neutral | negative",
}

def transcribe_audio(audio_path: str) -> dict:
    """Step 1: Convert audio to transcript using Whisper."""
    with open(audio_path, "rb") as f:
        transcript = client.audio.transcriptions.create(
            model="whisper-1",
            file=f,
            response_format="verbose_json",  # includes word timestamps
            timestamp_granularities=["word"],
        )
    return {
        "text": transcript.text,
        "language": transcript.language,
        "duration": transcript.duration,
    }


def extract_fields(transcript_text: str) -> dict:
    """Step 2: Feed transcript to a text LLM for structured extraction."""
    system_prompt = (
        "You are a support call analyst. Extract structured data from the "
        "provided call transcript. Return ONLY a JSON object matching this schema:\n"
        + json.dumps(EXTRACTION_SCHEMA, indent=2)
    )
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
            {"role": "user", "content": f"Transcript:\n{transcript_text}"},
        ],
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        temperature=0,
    )
    return json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)


def process_call(audio_path: str) -> dict:
    transcript_data = transcribe_audio(audio_path)
    extracted = extract_fields(transcript_data["text"])
    return {
        "transcript": transcript_data["text"],
        "language": transcript_data["language"],
        "duration_seconds": transcript_data["duration"],
        **extracted,
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = process_call("support_call.wav")
    print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
Example 1: Transcribe and detect language with Whisper
This minimal example transcribes a file and auto-detects the spoken language, which is useful for routing multilingual calls to the correct downstream agent.

import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()

def detect_language_and_transcribe(audio_path: str) -> dict:
    with open(audio_path, "rb") as f:
        result = client.audio.transcriptions.create(
            model="whisper-1",
            file=f,
            response_format="verbose_json",
        )
    return {
        "language": result.language,          # e.g. "spanish"
        "transcript": result.text,
        "duration_s": result.duration,
    }

info = detect_language_and_transcribe("customer_query.mp3")
print(f"Language: {info['language']}")
print(f"Text: {info['transcript'][:200]}...")
Example 2: Native audio with GPT-4o Audio Preview

This example sends raw audio bytes directly to GPT-4o Audio Preview, which processes audio natively. The model can detect tone and urgency from the acoustic signal, not just the words — useful for prioritizing escalations.
Contact center post-call analytics at scale

A mid-sized telecom processes 200,000 support calls per day. Each call averages 8 minutes. The previous workflow required a 24-hour delay for random manual sampling (5% of calls). With audio prompting, the goal is 100% call analysis within 30 minutes of call completion.
Deepgram Nova-2 over Whisper because it supports real-time diarized streaming at lower latency and is priced per second with a committed-use discount.
GPT-4o mini for extraction (lower cost, sufficient accuracy for structured fields) with GPT-4o reserved for escalation scoring where nuance matters.
Not needed — average call is 8 minutes, well within the 25-minute Whisper API limit and the context window for extraction.
200k calls/day = 2.3 calls/second. A pool of 50 async workers saturates the Whisper and GPT-4o mini APIs within rate limits.

import asyncio
import openai
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import AsyncIterator

client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """Analyze this customer support call transcript.
Return JSON: {
  "issue_type": "billing|technical|account|other",
  "resolved": true/false,
  "csat_predicted": 1-5,
  "escalation_risk": "low|medium|high",
  "verbatim_complaint": "string or null",
  "agent_script_adherence": true/false
}"""

@dataclass
class CallRecord:
    call_id: str
    audio_path: str


async def process_one(record: CallRecord) -> dict:
    # 1. Transcribe
    async with open(record.audio_path, "rb") as f:
        tr = await client.audio.transcriptions.create(
            model="whisper-1", file=f, response_format="text"
        )

    # 2. Extract
    resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
            {"role": "user", "content": tr},
        ],
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        temperature=0,
    )
    fields = json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)
    return {"call_id": record.call_id, **fields}


async def batch_process(records: list[CallRecord], concurrency: int = 50):
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)

    async def bounded(r):
        async with sem:
            return await process_one(r)

    results = await asyncio.gather(*[bounded(r) for r in records], return_exceptions=True)
    return [r for r in results if not isinstance(r, Exception)]

At 50 concurrent workers and roughly 15 seconds of API time per call, this pipeline processes 200,000 calls in about 17 hours of wall time — well within the 24-hour target. Moving to 150 workers with higher rate-limit tiers brings it under 6 hours.
Sending an audio file without specifying the language or format leads to the model spending tokens auto-detecting locale. For known-language corpora this is wasted compute and occasionally wrong.
send the file with no language parameter, let the model guess.
(Whisper) or include a system prompt line: "The caller speaks English with a Canadian accent.".

Splitting audio at fixed time intervals without overlap cuts sentences in half. The model cannot complete a thought that started in the previous chunk, producing garbled or missing information at boundaries.

# Wrong: hard cut every 5 minutes
for i in range(0, duration, 300):
    chunk = audio[i:i+300]  # sentence amputated at 300s

# Right: 15-second overlap + inject previous chunk summary
CHUNK = 300  # 5 minutes
OVERLAP = 15

for i in range(0, duration, CHUNK - OVERLAP):
    chunk = audio[i:i + CHUNK]
    # prepend: "Previous chunk ended with: {prev_summary}"
Asking the model to "summarize and extract the key points" produces inconsistent output shapes that are painful to parse. Always specify an explicit JSON schema in the system prompt and use
Not handling empty or failed transcripts

Very short audio clips (under 1 second), silent recordings, or recordings with no intelligible speech cause STT models to return empty strings. Downstream extraction code that assumes non-empty input will crash or produce hallucinated content.
Resample to 16 kHz mono, apply volume normalization (target -23 LUFS), and trim leading/trailing silence. This consistently reduces word error rates by 10–20%.

Define the exact JSON shape in the system prompt and validate the response against a schema (e.g., Pydantic) before persisting. Reject or flag responses that fail validation rather than silently storing malformed data.
When processing segmented audio, prepend a two-sentence summary of the previous chunk's content so the model can resolve pronouns and continue reasoning across boundaries.
Speaker labels ("Agent:", "Customer:") dramatically improve the accuracy of role-specific extractions like agent compliance checks or customer verbatim quotes.
STT APIs return word-level confidence scores. Flag transcripts where the average confidence falls below a threshold (e.g., 0.75) for human review rather than blindly trusting extraction output.

A single prompt that asks for transcription, summarization, sentiment, intent, and action items in one pass often degrades quality on all of them. Use separate, focused calls where accuracy matters most.
STT is deterministic for a given file. Cache the raw transcript so you can re-run different extraction prompts on the same recording without re-incurring STT cost.
Include crosstalk samples, non-native speaker accents, and noisy environments in your evaluation set. Aggregate word error rate on clean audio will be misleadingly optimistic.

For file-based workflows the dominant latencies are: file upload (proportional to size, typically 50–500ms for a 5-minute mono MP3), STT processing (1–3× real-time for cloud APIs, meaning a 5-minute clip takes 5–15 seconds), and LLM extraction (1–3 seconds for short transcripts with gpt-4o-mini). Total latency for a 5-minute call: roughly 10–20 seconds in the pipeline approach.

Native audio models (GPT-4o Audio Preview) reduce pipeline stages but do not always reduce wall-clock latency for file processing. The advantage of native models appears in streaming scenarios where the audio is produced and consumed simultaneously.
Audio processing is I/O-bound (upload + API wait). Use asyncio or a thread pool to run dozens of calls concurrently per process.
Publish completed calls to a queue (SQS, Pub/Sub). A fleet of workers consumes from the queue, making the system horizontally scalable and resilient to API rate-limit spikes.
Classify calls by importance first (fast, cheap model), then apply expensive deep analysis only to high-priority segments.
OpenAI's Batch API offers 50% cost reduction for non-time-sensitive audio processing by deferring to off-peak windows.
Approximate cost for 200,000 × 8-minute calls per day using the pipeline approach:

Switching to Deepgram Nova-2 (approximately $0.0043/minute with a committed-use discount) reduces STT cost to $6,880/day. The extraction LLM cost is relatively small and is dominated by STT for long recordings.
Excellent multilingual support, competitive accuracy. Self-hostable for data-sensitivity requirements. The whisper-large-v3 model achieves near-human accuracy on clean audio.
Optimized for telephone audio (8 kHz) and real-time streaming. Supports diarization and smart formatting out of the box.
Strong diarization, chapter detection, PII redaction, and sentiment analysis as built-in post-processing steps — useful for reducing the number of separate LLM calls.
Tight integration with Google Cloud; chirp model supports 100+ languages.
Strong compliance story (data residency, HIPAA BAA) for regulated industries.
Accepts audio input natively via the Chat Completions API. Supports streaming output. Best for tasks requiring paralinguistic reasoning.
Accepts audio files up to approximately 9.5 hours in a single context window. Cost-effective for long recordings.
As of mid-2025, Claude does not natively ingest audio. The recommended pattern is STT → Claude for extraction/reasoning.

# pydub: resample, normalize, chunk audio
from pydub import AudioSegment
from pydub.effects import normalize

audio = AudioSegment.from_file("call.mp3")
audio = audio.set_channels(1).set_frame_rate(16000)  # mono 16kHz
audio = normalize(audio)  # peak normalize

# Export 5-minute chunks with 15s overlap
CHUNK_MS = 5 * 60 * 1000
OVERLAP_MS = 15 * 1000
for i, start in enumerate(range(0, len(audio), CHUNK_MS - OVERLAP_MS)):
    chunk = audio[start:start + CHUNK_MS]
    chunk.export(f"chunk_{i:03d}.wav", format="wav")
Audio prompting is one of several strategies for extracting value from spoken content. The choice depends on accuracy requirements, latency, cost, and infrastructure constraints.

Audio recordings are biometric data under GDPR, CCPA, and BIPA in addition to containing spoken PII (full names, account numbers, addresses, health information). Before sending recordings to any third-party API, verify that the vendor's data processing agreement covers the applicable regulatory requirements. Many enterprises require that audio be processed in a specific geographic region or on a dedicated private endpoint.

Consider PII redaction at the transcript level (not the audio level) when using the pipeline approach: an STT engine with built-in redaction (AssemblyAI, Azure) masks sensitive entities before the transcript leaves the first service, so the downstream LLM never sees raw PII.

STT models hallucinate. The most common failure modes are: substituting a plausible common word for an unusual proper noun ("John Smith" → "Jon's myth"), inserting words during long silences, and omitting mumbled or overlapping speech. For high-stakes applications (legal, medical), include a confidence-score threshold below which the system flags for human review rather than auto-processing.

def is_reliable(transcript_json: dict, min_confidence: float = 0.75) -> bool:
    """Check Whisper verbose_json word confidence scores."""
    words = transcript_json.get("words", [])
    if not words:
        return False
    avg_conf = sum(w.get("probability", 0) for w in words) / len(words)
    return avg_conf >= min_confidence
Set a per-chunk timeout; implement retry with exponential backoff; fall back to a secondary STT provider.
Implement token-bucket rate limiting in the worker pool; queue excess calls rather than dropping them.
Validate audio duration and RMS amplitude before submission; route sub-threshold files to a dead-letter queue for investigation.
Wrap JSON parsing in try/except; retry with a stricter system prompt that emphasizes valid JSON; log the raw response for debugging.
Never log raw audio bytes or complete transcripts to general-purpose logs. Use a dedicated audit log with restricted access and retention policies aligned to data classification.

Audio prompting can follow two paths: a dedicated STT engine feeding a text LLM (lower cost, modular) or a native multimodal model (paralinguistic awareness, fewer pipeline stages); choose based on whether prosodic signals matter.
Preprocessing — resampling to 16 kHz mono, normalizing amplitude, and trimming silence — consistently reduces word error rates and should always be applied before submission.
Long recordings must be chunked with overlapping boundaries (typically 10–15 seconds) and a cross-chunk context injection to prevent information loss at segment edges.
Always specify the output schema explicitly in the prompt and use structured output modes (JSON response format) to ensure parseable, consistent responses rather than free-form prose.
Cache raw transcripts: STT is deterministic per file, so re-running different extraction prompts on the same recording should not re-incur transcription cost.

Audio recordings are biometric data subject to GDPR, CCPA, and BIPA; validate that vendor data-processing agreements cover all applicable regulations and consider transcript-level PII redaction before data leaves the first service.
Monitor transcript confidence scores and route low-confidence outputs to human review; STT hallucinations on names and jargon are common and can silently corrupt downstream structured data.

For high-throughput batch pipelines, use async concurrent workers behind a message queue, apply tiered processing (cheap model first, expensive model for escalations), and explore batch API pricing for off-peak cost reduction.
